Summary: ismembertol and uniquetol leak memory
Description: ismembertol  leaks memory for all input combinations. uniquetol  leaks memory when the first input has more than one column and the 'ByRows'  name-value pair is not specified.
Workaround: To work around this issue, use the below functions instead of ismembertol or uniquetol:function [C,IA,IC] = myuniquetol(varargin)[C,IA,IC] = builtin('uniquetol',varargin{:});endfunction [LIA,LOCB] = myismembertol(varargin)[LIA,LOCB] = builtin('ismembertol',varargin{:});endYou can alternatively name these replacement functions ismembertol and uniquetol to override the built-in functions with the replacement functions.?However, this generates a warning about a name conflict.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Missing assignment to exported function output
Description: In generated code, the output of a Stateflow exported function is returned but incorrectly not assigned to a variable when all of the following conditions are true: The exported function is called from another chart? Allow exported functions to be called by Simulink ?is enabled for the calling chart? Allow exported functions to be called by Simulink ?is disabled for?the callee chart The function packaging for the callee chart is set to "Auto" or "Reusable Function"

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Compiling generated code can throw a macro re-definition warning
Description: Embedded Coder throws a "macro re-defined"  warning when compiling the generated code of a model with all these properties: CustomSymbolStrMacro  set to a non-default value with length equal to MaxIdLength - 7  InlinedPrmAccess  set to Macro   The model may also contain blocks like Data Store Memory and Discrete Integrator.
Fixed In: R2016b Update 7, R2017a Update 4, R2018b Update 1, R2019a

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated AUTOSAR C code uses uninitialized local variables for explicit writes
Description: An AUTOSAR top model generates RTE write function calls that use uninitialized local variables if all of the following conditions are true: The model writes outputs of a referenced model to root outports. The root outports are configured for AUTOSAR data access mode ExplicitSend . Model initialization for the referenced model does not initialize outputs.  Under these conditions, the generated initialization code for the top model contains uninitialized stack variables, which RTE write function calls incorrectly write to root outports.
Workaround: Insert Signal Conversion blocks between the referenced model outputs and the root outports. In each Signal Conversion block, set Output to Signal copy (the default) and select the option Exclude this block from 'Block reduction' optimization.

Summary: Simulink Web View shows a blank page when viewed on Google Chrome version 68 or later
Description: Simulink Web View generated in Simulink Report Generator versions R2014b through R2016a shows a blank page when viewed on Google Chrome versions 68 and later. This error appears on the Google Chrome console:? Uncaught ReferenceError: normal is not defined
Workaround: Installation instructions  Log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.  Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps. On Mac systems, the folder name will have a .app extension, e.g. /Applications/MATLAB_R20XXa.app  Exit MATLAB.  Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Windows Systems: You may need administrative privileges to save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder. To do this, open this Bug Report in a browser started in administrator mode, then from the browser save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.Mac Systems: If you are using Safari on a Mac, disable automatic unzipping of the downloaded file by unchecking Open Safe Files After Downloading in Preferences > General.   For each file that appears in the .zip file, find the corresponding file in your existing <MATLAB> folder and make a backup of it. To see the contents of the .zip file:          Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.      Linux/Mac Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Mac):        unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>On a Mac, include the .app extension in the folder name.         Extract the files from the .zip file into the <MATLAB> folder.          Windows Systems:                  Open the .zip file.          Extract the contents of the file into your <MATLAB> folder. You may be prompted to provide Administrator privileges when extracting the .zip file.                    Linux/Mac Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Mac):         cd <MATLAB>        unzip <.zip file>On a Mac, include the .app extension in the folder name.        Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.To avoid having to re-export your models, run these commands after installing the above workaround.Update the exported Web View zip file, where webviewZipFile is the file path to an existing Simulink Web View zip file.   update_slwebview(webviewZipFile) Update the exported Web View HTML, where webviewFolder is the folder path to an existing Simulink Web View.   update_slwebview(webviewFolder)

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Lookup Table (n-D) blocks incorrectly share same lookupnd function?for some heterogeneous fixed-point inputs and floating-point breakpoints
Description: Lookup Table (n-D) blocks might incorrectly share the?same lookupnd  function?for some heterogeneous fixed-point inputs and floating-point breakpoints?when the following conditions are true for two or more Lookup Table?(n-D) blocks in a model: The breakpoints are double or single floating-point data types. The inputs are fixed-point data types with same?container word length, different?fixed exponent.   The incorrectly shared lookupnd  function might lead to wrong results?from the generated code in accelerator and rapid accelerator modes, and in all production targets.
Workaround: Cast the fixed-point input?of Lookup n-D blocks to have the same floating-point data type as breakpoint, i.e., insert a data type conversion block before the?input?of Lookup n-D block that generates wrong result.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Mismatch in behavior with MATLAB when using element-wise multiplication (.*) that mixes double and int64 or uint64 inputs
Description: The generated code can produce a wrong answer if your MATLAB code contains an element-wise multiplication?(.*) ?that mixes?double ?and?int64 ?or?uint64 ?inputs. This issue occurs?when all of the following conditions are true:? One input is?double ?and the other is?either?int64 ?or?uint64.  The?double ?input has absolute value greater than or equal to 2^52.  The? int64 ?or?uint64  input has absolute value less than  2^12.   This issue affects MATLAB Coder, MATLAB Function blocks and MATLAB System blocks in Simulink, and MATLAB functions in Stateflow. See also Bug Report ID 1794275 .

Summary: Slow model compilation for output signals nested in large virtual buses
Description: Update diagram, simulation, and code generation may be slow when an atomic subsystem or model has an output signal nested in a large virtual bus. These processes may display status "Compiling: Setting up implicit testpoints: Completed"  for longer than expected. These conditions may contribute to slow model compilation: Specifying?'inf'  for the sample time of a Constant block.  Having this Constant block connect directly?to an Outport block in a conditional subsystem.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Using Discrete State-Space block with state name defined as xnew can lead to incorrect answer during code generation
Description: If a model has Discrete State-Space block, incorrect code is generated when all these conditions are met: State name  is xnew .?The name xnew  conflicts with the TLC keyword. Code Generation storage class  is ExportedGlobal , ImportedExtern  or any Custom storage class.
Workaround: Change the State name.
Products: Simulink Coder, Embedded Coder, MATLAB Coder
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect results are possible for cascading Unit Delay blocks inside Switch Case Action Subsystem block
Description: The state for a Unit Delay block may incorrectly be a local variable in the generated code for a model that meets all of these conditions:  The Unit Delay blocks are inside a Switch Case Action Subsystem block. The output of one Unit Delay block feeds into another Unit Delay block. The Unit Delay block outputs are vectors or a matrices. In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, the Enable local block outputs  parameter is selected.
Workaround: Unzip the attachment and save the workaroundForBugReport1796701.p file to a folder on your MATLAB path. Run this script in each MATLAB session before opening your model.?
Fixed In: R2016b Update 7, R2017a Update 3, R2017b Update 8, R2018a Update 5, R2018b, R2019a

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Mismatch in behavior with MATLAB when using element-wise multiplication (.*)?that mixes?double and int64 or uint64 inputs
Description: The generated code can produce a wrong answer if your MATLAB code contains an element-wise multiplication (.*) ?that mixes double  and int64  or uint64  inputs. This issue occurs?when all of the following conditions are true:? One input is double  and the other is?either int64  or uint64.  The double  input has absolute value less than 2^-12.   This issue affects MATLAB Coder, MATLAB Function blocks and MATLAB System blocks in Simulink, and MATLAB functions in Stateflow. See also Bug Report ID 1849460
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Write to variable missing when variable is size vector argument of reshape function call within a loop
Description: The code generator might incorrectly omit writes to a variable when all of the following conditions occur:The variable is the size vector argument of a reshape  function call. The reshape  function call exists within a loop. There are writes to every element of the variable within the same loop  When this bug occurs, writes to the variable preceding the loop might be incorrectly missing in the generated code. For an example, see the function issue_example_1793805 below.function Y = issue_example_1793805(X,initSize,finalSize)    assert(isa(X,'double'));    assert(all(size(X) == [10,10,10]))    assert(isa(initSize,'int32'));    assert(all(size(initSize) == [1,3]));    assert(isa(finalSize,'int32'));    assert(all(size(finalSize) == [1,3]));    initSize(2) = initSize(2)*initSize(3);    initSize(3) = int32(1);    Y = zeros(numel(initSize),1);    for k = 1:numel(initSize)        if(k == 1 || k == numel(initSize))            Y(k) = locProcess(int32(reshape(X,initSize)));        end         initSize(k) = finalSize(4 - k);    endendfunction out = locProcess(in)    coder.inline('never');    out = size(in,2)*size(in,3) + size(in,1) * sum(in(:));end  The generated code for this function is as follows:void issue_example_1793805(const double X[1000], int initSize[3], const int? finalSize[3], double Y[3]){? int i;? emxArray_int32_T *b_X;? int i0;? int b_initSize[3];? int loop_ub;? double d0;? int i1;? (void)initSize;? for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {??? Y[i] = 0.0;? }? emxInit_int32_T(&b_X, 3);? for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {  ? ?? [SNIP]   The writes corresponding to the statements 'initSize(2) = initSize(2)*initSize(3)'  and 'initSize(3) = int32(1);'? are not in the generated code. These writes are necessary for the behavior of the generated code to match MATLAB. Instead the code generator should produce this code. Note the assignments 'initSize[1] = (int)i0;' and ' initSize[2] = 1;'. void issue_example_1793805(const double X[1000], int initSize[3], const int? finalSize[3], double Y[3]){? long i0;? int i;? emxArray_int32_T *b_X;? int i1;? int loop_ub;? double d0;? int i2;? i0 = (long)initSize[1] * initSize[2];? if (i0 > 2147483647L) {??? i0 = 2147483647L;? } else {??? if (i0 < -2147483648L) {????? i0 = -2147483648L;??? }? }? initSize[1] = (int)i0;? initSize[2] = 1;? for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {??? Y[i] = 0.0;? }? emxInit_int32_T(&b_X, 3); ? for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {  ? [SNIP]    This issue can happen for these code generation workflows:MATLAB Coder only workflow Simulink code generation of a model containing a MATLAB Function Block, Matlab System Block, or Stateflow Chart
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: The simulation of Direct Lookup Table (n-D) block outputs wrong result for some 1-D tables
Description: The simulation?of Direct Lookup Table (n-D) block outputs wrong?results when all of these conditions are met: Table is from input port.  Table is a 1-D vector with 2-D dimension (for example, N  x 1 or 1 x N ), but is not interpreted as a 1-D vector. The index port input value is greater than the table dimension size. The Inputs select this object from table  parameter of the block is set as Element .  In R2013b and earlier releases, both simulation and code generation output wrong results?for these?conditions.? Starting in?R2014a, the code generation output is correct for these conditions, but the simulation output is wrong.
Workaround: If the table is input from a Constant block,?select the Interpret vector parameter as 1-D check box for the?Constant block.Otherwise:Insert a Reshape block, with the Output dimensionality parameter set to?1-D array,?between the Table input port (T)?of the Direct Lookup Table (n-D) block and the?block that feeds the table port T.For an example of this workaround, see the attached model.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Interpolation using Prelookup block extracts wrong elements when selection port (sm) has negative values
Description: Incorrect code is generated from the Interpolation using Prelookup block under these conditions:  The Specification  parameter is set to Lookup table object  The Lookup table object?has Breakpoints Specification  set to?'Reference'  Each Simulink.Breakpoint  object has the Support tunable size  check box selected The Number of sub-table selection dimensions ?is greater than 0 Input to the selection port is a negative value   These conditions cause?incorrect elements to be output?from the table data?of the Simulink.LookupTable  object.
Workaround: Between the input source and the selection port (sm) of the Interpolation Using Prelookup block, add a Data Type Conversion block, whose Output data type is unsigned int,? with the following settings:Saturate on integer overflow check box is selectedOutput Minimum is set?to 0Output data type is set to the corresponding unsigned int:If input is?int8 - output?uint8If input is?int16 - output?uint16If input is?int32 - output?uint32

Summary: Code and Simulation mismatch when Simulink Function is in an algebraic loop
Description: The behavior of the code generated for a model may not match the behavior of a simulation of the model?when all of following conditions are true:? The model contains a Simulink Function block An output port of the Simulink Function block is connected directly to an input port of that Simulink Function block Inside the Simulink Function, the Outport block corresponding to that Simulink Function?block output port is connected directly to an ArgIn  port
Workaround: Place a Signal Conversion block after the ArgIn port and select the Exclude this block from 'Block reduction' optimization checkbox.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 3, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Simulink Function blocks?that specify shared input/output argument might reuse wrong input in generated code
Description: If a Simulink Function block with input/output reuse has any another input with the same datatype, complexity, and size as the reused input, the generated code might call the?function?with incorrect arguments. Specifically, the generated code might reuse the wrong variable at the Function Caller block?and produce a wrong answer. This occurs even if the other inputs are also specified to reuse with another output. In the below example, the?Simulink Function?f  has a 2 inputs u ?and x , and two outputs y ?and x .? The input/output?have the same?name, so?a single argument?is used in the function for the input/output pair. The generated code for the function definition looks similar to the following code: /* Output and update for Simulink Function: '<Root>/Simulink Function' */  void f(real_T rtu_u, real_T *rty_y, real_T *rtuy_x)  {  ? ...  ?The problem occurs at the function call site.? Here is an example of incorrect code: ? ...  ? /* FunctionCaller: '<Root>/Function Caller' */  ? rtb_inoutarg = rtb_input2;  ? f(rtb_input2, &rtb_output1, &rtb_inoutarg);  ? ... ? The first statement occurs because Simulink cannot reuse the same variable on the shared input/output -- a copy of the input is introduced.? The problem is that the two copies of input x  are passed to the Simulink function as inputs x  and u . ?The correct code uses a different variable for input u  and appears as follows: ? ...  ? /* FunctionCaller: '<Root>/Function Caller'? */  ??rtb_inoutarg?=?rtb_input2;  ? f(rtb_input1, &rtb_output1, &rtb_inoutarg);  ? ...
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 6, R2018a Update 2, R2018b

Summary: Unused local variables in model initialization function
Description: Simulink Coder can generate unused local variables in the model_initialize  function under these following conditions: The model contains a user written S-function that generates C/C++ code utilizing a TLC implementation. The user written S-function implements TLC based expression folding. The user written S-function TLC implementation has a block initialization function.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 6, R2017a Update 3, R2018a Update 4, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: The bus elements of a variant virtual bus?are unconditionally generated in the code
Description: Variant bus signals with? GeneratePreprocessorConditionals? selected ?are unconditionally generated in the code when all of the below conditions are true: A subsystem containing a bus selector block has a? different variant condition than the incoming virtual bus. The option  Treat as grouped when propagating variant conditions ? or?Treat As Atomic Unit ? is selected for the subsystem.  Both the parent bus and the child signals get selected using the bus selector within the subsystem.  The child bus signals have an output port connection. The parent bus is left unconnected.
Fixed In: R2016b Update 6, R2017b Update 8, R2018a Update 4, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect variable reuse when a Model block or Probe block connects to a Unit Delay block
Description: When a Model block or Probe block connects to a Unit Delay block, the state variable may be incorrectly reused if the following conditions are true: In the case of the Model block, inside the Model block, the root outport block that drives the Unit Delay block has non-zero initial value (for example, it is driven by a Constant block) that is different from the initial value of the state variable.  In the case of the Probe block, the Probe block connects to a Unit Delay block, and the initial value is not equal to the signal size that the Probe block acts on.  Optimize global data access  configuration parameter is set to Use global to hold temporary results or Minimize global data access ; or Reuse global block outputs  configuration parameter is enabled.  Reuse local block outputs ?configuration parameter is enabled.  When these conditions are true, the model block output or Probe block output and Unit Delay state may get merged, resulting in an incorrect answer because the mismatched initial conditions were merged.?
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 6, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Symbolic dimension specified for Output Size of Selector block is ignored in the generated code
Description: When generating code for a model containing a Selector block whose Output Size is specified using a symbolic dimension, Embedded Coder omits the symbolic specification and instead inlines the numeric value.?
Fixed In: R2016b Update 7, R2017b Update 8, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Uninitialized local variables generated with Variant Subsystem when CombineOutputUpdateFcns is selected in Configuration Parameters
Description: For ERT-based targets, uninitialized local variables may be generated in code for Simulink models with Variant Subsystem when all the following conditions are met:  The variant subsystem has the GeneratePreprocessorConditionals  parameter selected.   Configuration Parameters> Code Generation >Interface ?pane>Single output/update function  is selected. A direct feedback loop exists from an output port of the variant subsystem to the input port.  A variant subsystem choice involved in the direct feedback loop consistently breaks the loop from within the choice itself.  A variant subsystem choice not involved in the direct feedback?loop has a state update block (example,Unit Delay) connected to a block capable of creating local variable.
Workaround: Any of following workarounds help avoiding the incorrect behavior:In the Code Generation > Interface pane, disable Single output/update function.Ensure that the algebraic loop is not broken from within the variant subsystem choice.Break the algebraic loop by connecting the Unit Delay block between the output and input of the variant subsystem.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code generation?might occur for S-Functions that use?ssSetInputPortOverWritable
Description: For an S-Function that sets?ssSetInputPortOverWritable  and has multiple input ports, reuse different than the specification might occur?causing incorrect generated code.  To trigger this bug, the?S-Function must meet these conditions: S-Function block output must be set to SS_REUSABLE_AND_LOCAL.  At least one S-Function input port must be set to overwritable. Output port must set a specific input port to overwrite?another specific?input port. One other input port must have the same datatype and size as one of the input ports in the overwrite specification.   For example, a particular block with two inputs may specify that only the second output should overwrite the second input using these functions: ? ?? ?ssSetOutputPortOptimOpts(S, 0, SS_REUSABLE_AND_LOCAL);  ? ? ssSetOutputPortOptimOpts(S, 1, SS_REUSABLE_AND_LOCAL);  ? ? ssSetInputPortOverWritable(S, 0, 0); // First input port not overwritable  ? ? ssSetInputPortOverWritable(S, 1, 1); // Second input port is overwritable  ? ? ssSetOutputPortOverwritesInputPort(S, 0, -1); // First output port can't overwrite  ? ? ssSetOutputPortOverwritesInputPort(S, 1, 1); // Second output port can overwrite second input port  The generated code may end up sharing the first output port variable?with the second input port variable instead. ?If the block implementation writes the first output port before the second input port is read, an incorrect answer occurs.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code generated for the n-D Lookup table block with Interpolation method?Nearest?and Remove protection against out-of-range input in generated code?check box selected
Description: Incorrect?code for the n-D Lookup table block can generate incorrect output when all of the following conditions are true:? The Interpolation method ?is set to Nearest  The Index search method ?is set to Linear   search  The Remove protection against out-of-range input in generated code ?check box is selected The value of any input is nearest?to the last index value ofits breakpoints
Workaround: Do one of the following:Set the?Index search method?to be?Binary?searchClear?the?Remove protection against out-of-range input in generated code?check boxReplace the n-D Lookup Table block with one or more Prelookup blocks connected to?an Interpolation Using Prelookup block?and match the corresponding settings
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 6, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect code may be generated for fraction output of Prelookup block?in models containing multiple similar Prelookup blocks
Description: Incorrect code may be generated for the?fraction output of Prelookup blocks?under these conditions Model contains at least two Prelookup blocks Index search method  of each Prelookup block is set to Evenly spaced points  Data type of input signal and Breakpoints  data type?are not the same Breakpoints  data type of each block is a fixed-point data type The word length of?one or more?fixed-point data type differs from the word length of the other Breakpoints  data types The word length of each of the fixed-point data types must have the same container word length
Workaround: Set the word length of?all Prelookup Breakpoints data types to the same?length.

Summary: Simulation or code generation from a Simulink model?might cause MATLAB to crash
Description: In rare cases, MATLAB might crash for a Simulink model while you are performing either of these actions: Downloading code to an Arduino?platform using a support package Generating code using Simulink Coder  When the crash occurs, the stack trace might contain the message: Unhandled C++ exception in m2t_dmr_runtime:mf::dmr::Repository::Repository.
Workaround: You can avoid the crash by completing one of these actions:Remove all code generation artifacts from your current working directory.Navigate to a different folder.Clear the temporary directory.

Summary: Computer shuts down or does not respond when simulating a Simulink Desktop Real-Time model
Description: Real-time simulation of a Simulink Desktop Real-Time model might fail to start, report a timeout, or cause the computer to shutdown or restart?after updates are applied to the Windows operating system, or after the system has been upgraded to a newer release of Windows 10.

Summary: Incorrect handling of matrix concatenation in a MATLAB Function block under strict conditions
Description: Incorrect code generation might occur when using matrix concatenation in a MATLAB Function Block. For this to occur, all of the folowing conditions must be true: ?1.? Matrix concatenation is used on the right-hand side of an assignment. ?2. The matrix concatenation uses a variable that is the same as or depends on the variable used on the left-hand side of the assignment. ?3. The use of the variable from condition 2 appears in an expression such that a new value is produced. For example, if the variable from condition 2 is A, then A + A would be an expression that produces a new value.? ?4. The expression from condition 3 is indexed, either directly or indirectly through a new variable, such that a single element is extracted.? ?For example, consider the following MATLAB code:? function [y1,y2,y3] = foo(in)    y2 = 44;    y3 = 45;        y1 = in(6:3:33, 1:1:1);    P7 = (y1) + (y1);    y1(2:1:10, 1:1:1) = [in(3:6:39, 1:1:1); y1(4, 1); P7(5, 1)];end? Condition 1 is satisfied by: y1(2:1:10, 1:1:1) = [in(3:6:39, 1:1:1); y1(4, 1); P7(5, 1)]; Condition 2 is satisfied by both? y1(4, 1) and P7(5, 1). The latter satisfies condition 2 because P7 depends on y1. Condition 3 is satisfied by? P7 = (y1) + (y1); Condition 4 is satisfied by? P7(5, 1). ?The generated code for this example might be:? ? ? ?--SNIP--? ? ? rtb_P3 = 44.0;? rtb_P5 = 45.0;    for (i = 0; i < 10; i++) {    rtb_P1[i] = xg1716065_model_P.in_IA1_1[3 * i + 5];  }  tmp = rtb_P1[3];  for (i = 0; i < 7; i++) {    rtb_P1[1 + i] = xg1716065_model_P.in_IA1_1[6 * i + 2];  }  rtb_P1[8] = tmp;  rtb_P1[9] = rtb_P1[4] + rtb_P1[4]; ? ? ? --SNIP--?? The addition rtb_P1[4] + rtb_P1[4]  is incorrectly performed after the second loop. In the correct code, the addition is computed and stored in a temporary variable tmp_0  before the second loop:? ? ? ? --SNIP--? ?   rtb_P3 = 44.0;  rtb_P5 = 45.0;  for (i = 0; i < 10; i++) {    rtb_P1[i] = xg1716065_model_P.in_IA1_1[3 * i + 5];  }  tmp = rtb_P1[3];  tmp_0 = rtb_P1[4] + rtb_P1[4];  for (i = 0; i < 7; i++) {    rtb_P1[1 + i] = xg1716065_model_P.in_IA1_1[6 * i + 2];  }  rtb_P1[8] = tmp;  rtb_P1[9] = tmp_0;? ? ? ? --SNIP--? ?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Lookup Table?(n-D) or Prelookup blocks incorrectly share same prelookup function?for some fixed-point inputs and breakpoints
Description: The Lookup Table?(n-D) or Prelookup blocks might incorrectly share the same prelookup?function when the following condition is?true for two or more Lookup Table?(n-D) or Prelookup blocks in a model: Inputs and breakpoints both specify fixed-point data types with the?same?container word length , positive fixed exponent, and slope adjustment factor, but with different word length.  The incorrectly shared prelookup function might lead to wrong results?from the generated code.

Summary: Incorrect code may be generated for a For Iterator Subsystem containing an Assignment block with the input port <Y0> enabled
Description: Incorrect code may be generated for a model that meets these conditions: The model contains a For Iterator subsystem. The For Iterator subsystem contains an Assignment block with the input port <Y0>  enabled. The immediate data source feeding into the input port <Y0>  of the Assignment block is located outside the For Iterator subsystem. The aforementioned immediate data source is either a Mux block or a Bus Creator block configured to output a virtual bus signal.  In the incorrect generated C/C++ code, ?the variable corresponding to the output of the Assignment block is initialized by an uninitialized local variable.
Workaround: As a workaround, insert a Signal Conversion block with the Exclude this block from 'Block reduction' optimization parameter selected in front of the input port <Y0> of the Assignment block.

Summary: Incorrect preprocessor conditionals generated when Simulink.LookupTable object?containing fixed-point data?uses dimension variants
Description: When you use dimension variants with a Simulink.LookupTable   object containing fixed-point data, Embedded Coder can generate?incorrect preprocessor conditional statements. The incorrect generation occurs under?these two?scenarios. Scenario 1 ? If?a dimension symbol used in a lookup table object is a Simulink.Parameter  object named?symbolicDim, t  he incorrect generation occurs when all of these conditions are met:  A breakpoint set uses symbolicDim  as a dimension length.  The table data uses symbolicDim  as the length of the dimension corresponding to the breakpoint set. The table data uses a fixed-point data type while the breakpoint set uses a floating-point data type, or vice versa.  For the parameter object, suppose the  Min  property is set to 2  and Value  is set to 4 .  Under these conditions, the  expected preprocessor conditional is: #if symbolicDim <= 1  # error "The preprocessor definition?'symbolicDim' must be greater than '1'"?  #endif  However, the code generator produces:? #if symbolicDim !=?4  # error "The preprocessor definition 'symbolicDim' must be equal to '4'"?  #endif  Scenario 2   Suppose a breakpoint set and the table data both?use fixed-point data types. If the breakpoint set uses a dimension symbol breakpointDim  and the corresponding dimension of the table data uses a different?dimension symbol tableDim , the code generator omits an expected preprocessor conditional statement that confirms the equivalence of breakpointDim  and tableDim . The expected preprocessor conditional is: #if breakpointDim != tableDim?  # error?"The preprocessor definitions?'breakpointDim' and 'tableDim' must be equal"?  #endif?

Summary: MATLAB crashes during simulation or update diagram of a model hierarchy containing Variant Source blocks connected to global Data Store Read/Write blocks
Description: MATLAB may crash during simulation or update diagram of a model hierarchy if all of the following are true: A referenced model in the hierarchy has a global data store read block connected to an inactive input port of a Variant Source block The top model of the hierarchy has a global data store write block fed by an inactive Variant Source block. The Data Store Read/Write blocks specify the same Simulink.Signal object for the "Data store name" block parameter.??
Workaround: Turn off 'Analyze all choices during update diagram and generate pre-processor conditionals' for the Variant Source/Sink blocks in the model?Add Data Store Memory blocks to top and reference models referring to the commonly named data stores

Summary: State variable for Unit Delay incorrectly overwritten for a subsystem whose function interface includes arguments
Description: See?Bug Report ID 1668727 ?for full details on related issues.? Incorrect code generation might occur when all of the following conditions are true: Optimize global data access ?configuration parameter is set to?Use global to hold temporary results ?or?Minimize global data access  Reuse local block outputs ?configuration parameter is enabled. The model contains a subsystem in which the?Function packaging ?parameter set to?NonReusable?and Function interface ?is set to  Allow Arguments.   A Unit Delay block's input or output is connected to that subsystem's output signal.  Errors might be thrown during the build process.?
Workaround: See the workaround in?Bug Report ID 1668727.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2017b Update 4, R2018a

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible for AUTOSAR target when an S-function sets?ssSetOutputPortOutputExprInRTW?to?TRUE
Description: Incorrect code may be generated for a model when all these conditions are met:? The system target file is set to AUTOSAR.tlc, AND The system has an S-function with the ssSetOutputPortOutputExprInRTW(SimStruct *S, int idx, bool val)  function having the bool  argument val set to 1 (TRUE)? for at least one Outport block, AND The S-function must be connected to a root input port.  In the generated code, the input variable for the S-Function may be incorrectly reused.
Workaround: There are three workarounds, each of which will have impact on code efficiency (increasing stack size):Disable the configuration parameter Reuse local block outputs.?Set the bool?argument of ssSetOutputPortOutputExprInRTW to 0 (FALSE) for all the identified S-function blocks.??Insert a Signal Conversion block at the input of all affected S-Function blocks that set the bool argument of ssSetOutputPortOutputExprInRTW to 1 (TRUE). Enable the parameter?Excludethis block from ˇ®Block reductionˇŻ optimization on the Block Parameters?dialog box?for each insertedSignal Conversion block. Regenerate and inspect the code to confirm that theproblem is averted.?
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 6, R2017a Update 3, R2017b Update 6, R2018a

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code?can cause a numerical error for the Interpolation Using Prelookup block when using a Simulink.LookupTable object?with certain settings
Description: Incorrect code is generated in the Interpolation Using Prelookup block under these conditions: The Specification? parameter is set to?Lookup   table   object  The?Interpolation Method ?parameter is set to Flat  or Nearest  The Valid index input may reach last index ?check box is cleared  The source to the Interpolation Using Prelookup block?are Prelookup blocks Each Prelookup block has the?Specification? parameter set to Breakpoint object  Each Simulink.Breakpoint  object has the Support tunable size  check box selected  These conditions cause the output of an incorrect element from the Table   of the Simulink.LookupTable  object. Note that this?issue is encountered only when the Prelookup block outputs both: An index as the last index value of the breakpoints  A fraction?greater than or equal to 1
Workaround: As a workaround, select the:Valid index input may reach last index?check box of the Interpolation Using Prelookup blockUse last breakpoint for input at or above upper limit check box of?each Prelookup block?connected to the Interpolation Using Prelookup block

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated S-Function from a Simulink subsystem or model might crash MATLAB or return incorrect results
Description: An S-Function block generated from a subsystem or model might crash MATLAB or return incorrect results when it is used in a host model for simulation.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect Code Generation: Referenced model that includes Simulink function interfacing with local root inport signal and?used in?reusable top model can?produce incorrect code
Description: The code generator can produce incorrect code when using a referenced model that meets the following conditions:?? Contains a Simulink Function block.  In the local environment of the Simulink Function block, the block interfaces to a root inport signal of the model. Is used in a top model that is configured?with: Code interface packaging  set to Reusable function . Pass root-level I/O  set to Individual arguments . Multi-instance code error diagnostic  model parameter set to Warning  or None .   Under these conditions, generated top-model code passes a double reference to the input argument of the start function for the Simulink Function, instead of?the expected single reference input argument. For example, the code generator can produce the following incorrect code for this scenario. The call to Bot_Start , passes argument rtU_upper?as a double  ?reference. ?/* Model initialize function */ void Top_initialize(RT_MODEL *const rtM, real_T * rtU_upprt, real_T *rtY_output){? ? ?Bot_initialize(rtmGetErrorStatusPointer(rtM);? ? ?Bot_Start(&rtU_upper, rtY_output);? }? Instead, t he code generator  should produce  the follow ing code, where Bot_Start   passes rtU_upper   by a single reference. ? /* Model initialize function */ void Top_initialize(RT_MODEL *const rtM, real_T * rtU_upprt, real_T *rtY_output){? ? ?Bot_initialize(rtmGetErrorStatusPointer(rtM);? ? ?Bot_Start(rtU_upper, rtY_output);? }?
Workaround: For the top model that includes the reference model, make one of the following model configuration parameter changes:Set Pass root-level I/O to a value other than Individual arguments.Set Code interface packaging to Nonreusable function.

Summary: Variant conditions may guard shared utility code causing compilation error
Description: A compilation?error may occur for a model that meets these conditions: The?model contains at least one Variant block. In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, the Shared code placement ?parameter is set to Shared location .  The variant control variable is a Simulink.Parameter  with a Define  or an?ImportedDefine  custom storage class.  The compilation error occurs when code in the shared code folder has variant preprocessor conditionals.
Workaround: Change the setting of the Shared code placement parameter to Auto or for the variant control variables, change the custom storage class to CompilerFlag.

Summary: When an Export-function model is nested in more than three levels of harness models, a combination of using Constant sample time?might produce wrong answer
Description: An?atomic subsystem does not execute correctly in normal mode simulation and the wrong answer appears when all of the following?conditions are true:  There are at least three levels of model hierarchy  The bottom model is an Export-function model  There exists a constant block inside an atomic subsystem within a function-call subsystem in the Export Function model ?
Workaround: Run attached p code, exportedFcnWorkaround, in each MATLAB session before opening your model.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Code?replacement for Add/Subtract/Sum blocks connected with inline variants generates incorrect code
Description: Code replacement library generates incorrect code?when  all   of the following conditions are met:  The model contains one or more Add/Subtract/Sum blocks.   The model uses inline variants, and the inline variants (for example, Variant Source/Sink block)?are directly connected to the output of one or more Add/Subtract/Sum blocks.   The model that generates code uses a Code Replacement Library (CRL) that contains RTW_OP_ADD ? or? RTW_OP_MINUS  entries.   The input/output types on the Add/Subtract/Sum block?connected with the inline variants match the input/output types of the  RTW_OP_ADD   or  RTW_OP_MINUS   code replacement?entry. Code replacement occurs for the Add/Subtract/Sum blocks during code generation.
Workaround: Follow these guidelines when you generate code for a model that uses a custom code replacement library and contains Add/Subtract/Sum blocks as well as?inline variants:Do not?directly connect the output of an Add/Subtract/Sum block to a Variant Source/Sink block or other blocks that produce inline variants.Instead, connect the output of an Add/Subtract/Sum block to a No-Op block, and connect the output of the No-Op block to the Variant Source/Sink block or other blocks that produce inline variants.A No-Op block can be, but not limited to, one of?the followings:A Gain block with "Gain=1", and the properties "Output data type", "Saturate on integer overflow", "Integer rounding mode" all the same as the?properties on the Add/Subtract/Sum block.A Data Type Conversion block with the properties "Output data type", "Saturate on integer overflow", "Integer rounding mode" all the same as the?properties on the Add/Subtract/Sum block.A Signal Conversion block with "Output" property set to "Signal copy".

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Subsystem with State Reader or State Writer block generates wrong reusable function code
Description: If a Subsystem block contains a?State Reader or State?Writer block and the block parameter Function packaging  on the Code Generation tab is not set  t o  inline ,  Simulink generates wrong code. Reusable functions for subsystems containing State Reader or State?Writer blocks are not supported.?However, because there are no diagnostics for this problem, Simulink silently generates wrong code. The problem only happens with Subsystem that containing the State Reader Writer block but not the corresponding State Owner block. So if a subsystem containing both,  reusable function will work and the right dwork argument will pass in.For the subsystem that only contains State Reader Writer block but not the corresponding owner block,  our fix will disable the wrong reusable functions to be generated. In particular,  if customer specified "Inline" or "Nonreusable function",  we will honor the option; if customer specified "Auto",  we will make it inline; if customer specified "Reusable function",  we will change the generated code to be inline,  and report an error in the code generation report just as what Simulink does for other cases when reusable option cannot be honored.?
Workaround: Analyze the model?hierarchy for subsystems containing State Reader or State Writer blocks. Change the subsystem block parameter?Function packaging from Reusable function or Auto to Inline.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Casting tological from a fixed-point?type with slope bias scaling?that cannot represent zero canproduce the wrong answer
Description: In MATLAB, you candefine a fixed-point?type with slope bias scaling?that cannot represent zero and cast thistype to a logical type. In this operation, the correct logical result is true ,since the source value can never be zero. However, code generation produces false .This issue can occur in MATLAB Coder, or for a MATLAB Function block or a MATLAB System block in Simulink.  Here is an example that illustrates the issue:   x ?=cast(fi(1,?1,?32,?2,?-1),?'logical');?  This cast operationproduces true  in MATLAB, but generated code can compute false .?
Workaround: Avoidcasting to logical type for fixed-point types with slope bias scaling?that cannot representzero.

Table data type is different from input port data type of block succeeding Interpolation Using Prelookup  block.? Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code generation for an inlined MATLAB function that uses PARFOR loop
Description: MATLAB Coder can generate incorrect C code for a MATLAB function when both  of?these conditions are true: A parfor  loop in the?function modifies an output variable. Where the function is called, at the call site,?the output from the function is not assigned to a variable. Instead, it is assigned to a?structure field,?array element,?or similar expression.  In this case, if the code generator inlines the function, it?might incorrectly place the assignment from the function output on the left side of the assignment. Therefore, it?produces incorrect C code,?which results in a? wrong answer. This example illustrates the issue. This MATLAB code produces a different result in generated code than the result produced in MATLAB. function y = foo(n)z.a = zeros(n);  if n > 0? ? z.a = baz(n);end  y = z.a;end  function s = baz(n)coder.inline( 'always' );s = ones(n);parfor i = 1:n??? s(i) = i + 100;endend ?
Workaround: Place coder.inline('never') in functions that use parfor. For example:function y = foo(n)z.a = zeros(n);if n > 0? ? z.a = baz(n);endy = z.a;endfunction s = baz(n)coder.inline( 'never' );s = ones(n);parfor i = 1:n??? s(i) = i + 100;endend?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Possible wrong answer for code generated from function with multiple outputs and at least one pass-by-reference input
Description: Generated code might produce a different answer than MATLAB produces if all of the following conditions are true: A function has multiple outputs and at least one input argument that is passed by reference. For example:?  [a, b, c] = foo(a)  The pass-by-reference input and some of the outputs are not used after the function call.?
Workaround: If your MATLAB function has multiple outputs and a pass-by-reference input, use all of the outputs after the function call.

Summary: Code Generation Advisor check of model configuration settings generates error?

Configuration Parameters  > Optimization  > Use division for fixed-point net slope computation ?is set to Use division for reciprocals of integers only. Summary: Simulink may crash while running the generated code from a model with?Simulink.LookupTable or Simulink.Breakpoint objects that?contain fi objects
Description: Simulink may crash while running the generated code?from a model with one or both of these conditions:  An n-D Lookup Table block that contains a Simulink.LookupTable   object: The Specification  parameter of the Simulink.LookupTable   object set to:?either?Explicit values  or Reference . The?Breakpoints > Value  property of the object contains a vector specified in the form of a fi object The Support tunable size  check box in the object is selected.  A Prelookup block that contains a Simulink.Breakpoint  object: The?Breakpoints > Value  property of the object contains a vector specified in the form of a fi object.  The Support tunable size  check box in the object is selected.?   ?  ?
Workaround: As a workaround, separate the fi object specification?into the?Value and DataType properties of the Simulink.LookupTable or Simulink.Breakpoint?object, for example:These?functions set up?a Simulink.LookupTable object with a fi object specified in?the lookup table object?Breakpoints Value property. This configuration?may cause the issue:>>?lutObject = Simulink.LookupTable;>> lutObject.Breakpoints.Value= fi([1,2,3,4], fixdt(1,8,1,0));To resolve the issue, separate the fi object?specification into the?Value?and?DataType?properties: >> lutObject.Breakpoints.Value= [1,2,3,4];?>> lutObject.Breakpoints.DataType= 'fixdt(1,8,1,0)';

Summary: Signal Builder block generates index errors when replacing data using?the Import File dialog box

Summary: Root-level I/O?with reusable custom storage class specification and Inline Invariant Signals parameter selected may result in incorrect generated code
Description: The generated code may be incorrect for a model that meets the following conditions: The signals that connect to the root-level input and output?ports have the same reusable custom storage?specification. A Constant block connects to the root-level output port that has?the reusable custom storage class specification. In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the Optimization>Signals and Parameters  pane, the Inline Invariant Signals  parameter is selected.?
Workaround: To avoid this bug,?try one of the following:?1. On the Optimization>Signals and Parameters pane,?deselect the Inline Invariant Signals check box.2. Remove the reusable custom storage class specification on the root-level input and output ports.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible for noncontiguous reads and writes to the same matrix for which there is data dependency
Description: The generated code might be incorrect for multiple reads and writes to the same matrix that meets these conditions: The matrix reads and writes are noncontiguous.? There is data dependency between the matrix reads and writes.? For each dimension, all of the reads and writes access the same number of matrix elements. For each dimension, all of the reads and writes use the same stride between indexed elements.  In the incorrect generated code, a single, nested for  loop is generated?for the multiple reads and writes to the data. In the correct generated code, there should be separate, nested?for  loops to properly represent the data dependency. See the attached Simulink model ebr_example_1536107  for example modeling patterns that match the above conditions. ?
Workaround: To workaround this issue, modify the model so that the code generator places the?dependent reads and writes in separate functions.?If the reads and writes are spread across multiple Simulink blocks, you can achieve this by using an atomic subsystem that has the Function packaging parameter set to Reusable function?or Nonreusable function. For a MATLAB Function Block or MATLAB System Block, you?can achieve this by using functions with the specification coder.inline('never'). See the attached model ebr_workaround_1536107 for examples of these workarounds.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code is possible for single input/output Logic block with symbolic dimensions
Description: The generated code might be incorrect for a model that meets all of the following conditions:The model configuration parameter Allow symbolic dimension  specification is set to on . The model contains a single-input/single-output Logic block. The signal entering the Logic block has a specified or propagated dimension with a symbolic expression.  When the bug occurs, the symbolic expression is not fully preserved in the generated code for the Logic block.? In the generated code, the expression may be replaced with a numerical value based on the numerical value of the symbols at the time of code generation.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated code may be incorrect for a Constant block with a sample time other than inf that branches to multiple destinations
Description: The generated code may be incorrect for a Constant block that meets these conditions: The Constant block has a sample time other than inf .? The signal line that leaves the Constant block branches?to multiple destinations.? The Constant block?or one of its destinations has an asynchronous rate, or the?Constant block has a discrete rate, and one of its destinations has a faster discrete rate.?
Workaround: You can choose?either of these?two workarounds:In the Constant block parameters dialog box, set the Sample time parameter to Inf.??Place a Signal Conversion block at the output of the Constant block. In the Signal Conversion block parameters dialog box,?select Exclude this block from Block reduction optimization'.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible when the Casting Modes parameter is set to Standards Compliant
Description: Incorrect generated code is possible for a model that meets the following conditions:  In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the Code Style  pane, the Casting Modes  parameter is set to Standards Compliant . In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the Code Style  pane, the Allow right shifts on signed integers  parameter is selected and/or the Replace multiplications by powers of two with signed bitwise shifts  parameter is selected. A right shift or left shift occurs on a negative integer. A signed operation occurs on the result of the right shifted or left shifted operation.  In the incorrect generated code, a sign extension does not occur resulting in incorrect results.
Workaround: To workaround this issue, you can take one of?the following actions:?If you want to keep the Casting Modes parameter set to Standards Compliant, use the recommended MISRA settings by deselecting the Allow right shifts on signed integers parameter and the?Replace multiplications by powers of two with signed bitwise shifts parameter.Change the Casting Modes parameter setting?to Nominal or Explicit.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect logic in generated code when atomic subsystem has a single outport fed by a block conditionally writing to its output
Description: If you generate code for a model with an atomic subsystem in which all of the following conditions are true, the generated code might contain incorrect logic involving the subsystem output.In the Subsystem Block Parameters dialog box, on the Code Generation  tab, the Function packaging  parameter is set to Nonreusable function . The Function interface  parameter is set to Allows arguments .  The atomic subsystem has only one outport, and the signal leaving this outport is a scalar. The atomic subsystem outport is either fed by control flow logic in which not all possible execution paths are defined (for example, but not limited to, an If block without an else port, or a Switch Case block without a default port), or by a block that does not write to its output every time step.  Under these conditions a function might be generated for the atomic subsystem in which the output is passed by value, and that output is not initialized nor written to on all execution paths through the function.

Summary: Incorrect AUTOSAR Rte call order for IsUpdated inport that branches to nonreusable subsystem
Description: If an AUTOSAR model contains an inport that is configured for IsUpdated  data access mode, the generated code should call the Rte_IsUpdated  access method before its associated Rte_Read  access method.?If the calls are reversed, the Rte_Read  method clears the?Runtime Environment (RTE) IsUpdated  flag, which yields an invalid output. The Rte_IsUpdated  and Rte_Read  access methods might be reversed in generated code if all of the following conditions exist:   In an AUTOSAR model, two Simulink root inports are mapped to the same AUTOSAR port.?One inport is configured for ExplicitReceive  data access mode and the other for IsUpdated  data access mode. The inports are connected to a modeling pattern in which the inport with ExplicitReceive  access?can be read before the inport with?IsUpdated  access.? Examples include: Use of a Switch block. IsUpdated  inport has a higher Simulink port number than the ExplicitReceive  inport.  One of the following conditions pertaining to nonreusable subsystems exists: The IsUpdated  inport branches. One of its destinations is inside a nonreusable subsystem and another destination is outside the same nonresuable subsystem. In R2016b only, the IsUpdated  signal is inside a nonreusable subsystem and the AUTOSAR schema version configured for the model is lower than 4.0.??
Workaround: To work around this bug, make sure that the IsUpdated inport is read before its associated ExplicitReceive inport.For example, if the destination of the inport with IsUpdated data access is a Switch block, replace the Switch block with a masked block that contains the following:An if-else-action-merge system in which the condition is based on the first inport.Masked block I/O that matches the Switch block I/O.If the above action is not effective, examine port numbering.?For all inport blocks in the path between the IsUpdated/ExplicitReceive root inport pair and their final destination, make sure that IsUpdated inports always have a lower port number than their ExplicitReceive inport counterparts.

Summary: Saving a Simulink model with large subsystems can cause?the system to appear to become unresponsive
Description: If a Simulink model contains any subsystem whose dimension is over certain size, saving the model can take a very long time.?It can appear that the system has become unresponsive. The model will be saved after a few minutes without loss of?data. The size varies depending on system configurations. It's typically around 8000 x 4000 units. Not all models with large subsystems are affected.
Workaround: Turn off the Simulink preference for saving a?thumbnail image. See Simulink Preferences Model File Pane.

Summary: Code generation error when using Simulink.LookupTable or Simulink.Breakpoint?objects with reusable subsystem
Description: ?When you generate code from a model, Simulink Coder?can issue?an unexpected error when all of these conditions are true: The model contains CodeReuse Subsystem blocks. You explicitly?configure the subsystems for reuse in the generated code. Through a mask parameter, you pass?a?different Simulink.LookupTable  object to each subsystem. The objects exist in the base workspace.  The error also occurs when you use Simulink.Breakpoint  objects instead of Simulink.LookupTable  objects. ? ?

Summary: Model build may fail for Variant Subsystem containing blocks with Simulink.Parameters or mpt.Parameters with ImportedDefine storage class
Description: For a model?with a Variant Subsystem containing blocks that have Simulink.Parameters or mpt.Parameters  with an ImportedDefine  storage class,?the model build may fail because of an error pointing to undefined variables in the model_types.h  file. This error may occur because the Variant Control parameter definitions are in a separate header file or have different storage classes than the Simulink.Parameters or mpt.Parameters? specified for the block parameters.
Workaround: You can workaround this issue setting the storage class of variant control variables as ImportedDefine and placing all ImportedDefine variable definitions in the same header file.

Summary: Generated code might be?uncompilable for referenced models with MATLAB structure parameters
Description: Embedded Coder might generate uncompilable code for referenced models that meet the following conditions:  Child and parent models use the same parameter that is a MATLAB structure. In the child model, the structure elements are assigned to a Simulink.Bus  object with matching structure elements. This is the only reference to the Simulink.Bus  object in the model hierarchy.
Workaround: For the model parameter, use a Simulink.Parameter instead of a MATLAB structure . The Simulink.Parameter should have the MATLAB structure as its value and the Simulink.Bus object as its type.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible for a subsystem with a single output and nonreusable function packaging with arguments
Description: For a model that contains a subsystem that meets the following conditions, the code generator might incorrectly pass the subsystem output through the function return value instead of by reference: 1. The subsystem?has a single output. 2. In the Subsystem Block Parameters dialog box, on the Code Generation  tab, the Function packaging  parameter is set to Nonreusable function . The Function interface  parameter is set to Allows arguments . 3. Inside the subsystem, the subsystem output is conditionally written. 4. The subsystem output is a scalar.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect?generated code?is possible when Casting modes parameter is set to Standards Compliant and the model contains signed bitwise operands
Description: ?Incorrect generated code is possible for a model that meets the following conditions:   The model performs a bitwise operation using signed operands in a Stateflow chart, a MATLAB Function block,?or bitwise blocks.  One or more operands is negative.  The model contains signed fixed point data types that are larger than the target signed integer data type. The width of the fixed point data type does not match?the target built-in data type.   ? ?
Workaround: Change the setting of the Casting modes parameter to Nominal or Explicit.?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Nondeterministic data transfer in models with variant source connected between blocks operating at different rates
Description: Nondeterministic?data transfer during simulation and code generation for models when all the below conditions are true:  Variant source block is connected between blocks operating at different rates  Variant source block has  Analyze all choices during update diagram and generate preprocessor conditionals? option selected

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect Mfx_Div* replacements can occur in the generated code when using the AUTOSAR 4.0 CRL Library
Description: The following incorrect Mfx_Div*  replacements can occur when generating code using the AUTOSAR 4.0 CRL library:       In1 Type      In2 Type      Output Type      Incorrect Mfx_Div* replacement      Affected release           uint8      int32      uint16      Mfx_Div_s32u16_u16      R2014b - R2016b           int16      int32      int16      Mfx_Div_s32s16_s16      R2014b - R2016b           uint16      int32      uint16      Mfx_Div_s32u16_u16      R2014b - R2016b           uint8      uint32      uint16      Mfx_Div_u32u16_u16      R2014b           uint8      uint32      uint32      Mfx_Div_u32u16_u32      R2014b           uint16      uint32      uint16      Mfx_Div_u32u16_u16      R2014b           uint16      uint32      uint32      Mfx_Div_u32u16_u32      R2014b
Workaround: Visit Bug Report ID 1435708 to download a patch that addresses this and other bugs.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016a Update 7, R2016b Update 6, R2017a

Summary: During simulation, MATLAB can crash with the following message: ?LLVM ERROR / emlrtPeekStack
Description: Sometimes?during a simulation of a model that includes a MATLAB Function block, MATLAB can crash with the following message: LLVM ERROR: Program used external function 'emlrtPeekStack' which could not be resolved!?  ? ? ?
Workaround: 1) Download the ZIP file disable_emlrtstack.zip.2) Unzip it -- find the disable_emlrtstack.p file.3) After each MATLAB startup, run disable_emlrtstack. Alternatively, add this command to your startup.m file so that ?it always runs when MATLAB starts.With this?workaround, stack traces?might be?absent from certain run-time errors. Therefore, it can be?harder to find these errors in the model.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible for Rate Transition blocks which display buf with?Enable local block outputs parameter selected
Description: For a model containing a Rate Transition block, the critical section of the generated code that protects a global buffer may be unsafe under the following conditions:?? Code Generation flag condition:? In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the?All Parameter? tab, the?Enable local block outputs? parameter?is set to?on .?  ?Rate Transition block conditions:?   The input sample time of the Rate Transition block is?faster than the output sample time. The output sample time?is a multiple of the input sample time. In the Rate Transition block parameters dialog box, the Ensure data integrity during data transfer  parameter is selected. In the Rate Transition block?parameters dialog box, the Ensure deterministic data transfer (maximum delay)  parameter is not selected. The Rate Transition block displays buf  on the block icon because in?the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the Solver  pane, you select Automatically handle rate transition for data transfer  and Whenever Possible  or Never (minimum delay)  for Deterministic data transfer .?buf  signifies that a model contains such a type of Rate Transition block.   In the incorrect generated code, the global variable may be localized outside of the protection code.
Workaround: In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, on the All Parameters tab, set the Enable local block outputs parameter to off. Note that turning this parameter off may cause ROM consumption?to increase.

Summary: Unused variable compiler warning is possible for a model containing Variant Source or Variant Sink blocks
Description: The generated code may contain unguarded local variable definitions for a model containing Variant Source or Variant Sink blocks. These unguarded local variable definitions are possible for the following blocks: Model blocks S-functions Stateflow Charts or MATLAB Function blocks that?have Function Packaging  of Reusable function  Blocks for?viewing simulation results such as?Scope?and Display blocks  The warning?may state that a variable is unused or that a variable is set?but unused. The exact wording of the warning depends on the C compiler.
Fixed In: R2016a Update 7, R2016b Update 6, R2017a, R2017b

Summary: Uninitialized temporary variable generated for AUTOSAR model when subsystem output signal branches to root outport and subsystem inport
Description: ?An AUTOSAR model might generate an uninitialized temporary variable if both of the following conditions exist:  The model contains multiple subsystems with the same rate. The output signal of one subsystem connects to a root outport, and additionally branches to the inport of another subsystem.   In many (but not all) cases, the source subsystem is configured as nonreusable and the root outport is mapped to AUTOSAR data access mode ExplicitSend . Under these conditions, the generated C function code for the second, receiving?subsystem declares and uses an uninitialized temporary local variable.
Workaround: To work around this bug, do either of the following:Configure the subsystem that connects to the root outport to be reusable. For example, open the block parameters dialog box?for the subsystem and set Function packaging to Reusable function.On the signal branch that connects the subsystem to the root outport, insert a Gain block with gain value 1. Set?the storage class of the signal to be SimulinkGlobal. For example, right-click the signal?to the right of?the Gain block and?open the Signal Properties dialog?box. Enter a value for Signal name?and, in the Code Generation tab, set Storage class to?SimulinkGlobal.

Summary: CAN Transmit block does not stop periodic transmission
Description: When a CAN Transmit  block is configured for periodic transmission, either of the following should stop the transmission, but do not: The subsystem (such as an?Enabled Subsystem ) that contains the block becomes disabled. You pause the model execution.  Instead of stopping for either of these conditions as it should, the block transmission continues until the completion of the simulation.

Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.  Exit MATLAB.  Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report for your release, and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder. You might need administrative privileges to save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder. To do this, open this Bug Report in a browser started in administrator mode, then from the browser save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.   For each file that appears in the .zip file, find the corresponding file in your existing <MATLAB> folder and make a backup of it.   Extract the files from the .zip file into the <MATLAB> folder. You may be prompted to provide Administrator privileges when extracting the .zip file.  Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: Stateflow charts which call exported functions with bus types can sometimes result in a crash
Description: MATLAB can crash when you simulate or generate code for a model with a Stateflow chart that calls a function exported by another chart if all of the following conditions are met:  Some of the inputs or outputs of the function are of Simulink.Bus type. The caller chart passes in a local data (not a chart input or output) to the function. There are no other chart ?inputs or outputs with that data type.  The generated crash error log contains: ?failed assertion in b:\matlab\toolbox\stateflow\src\stateflow\cdr\cdr_transform_io_bus.cpp    ?
Workaround: Change the local data being passed to the exported function to a chart output instead. In order to prevent warnings in Simulink, connect this output to a terminator block.

Summary: Global data store value set to log in a function-call model reference crashes MATLAB
Description: MATLAB crashes during simulation?when both  of these conditions exist There is a global data store in a function-call model reference. The global data store is set for logging.?  ?

Summary: Code is incorrectly generated when Prelookup blocks are connected via buses with scalar expansion to an Interpolation Using Prelookup block configured for 2-D or higher
Description: Code is incorrectly?generated from a model with these configurations: Interpolation Using Prelookup block:Number of dimensions  - Set to 2  or higher  Require index and fraction as bus  - Selected   Multiple Prelookup blocks feeding the Interpolation Using Prelookup block:Output selection  - Set to Index and Fraction as bus  An input to one of the Prelookup blocks is scalar expanded
Workaround: As a workaround,?make all inputs to be vectors with the same dimension.?

Summary: Incorrect conditions guarding local variables result in compiler warnings
Description: Compiler warnings about unused variables are possible for a model that meets the following conditions:? The model contains a Variant Source block.? The?model contains a Root Inport block or?Constant block that drives a Variant Source block as its only input. The ground value is assigned to a local variable representing the output of the Variant Source block for the inactive variant.?  Preprocessor conditionals surround the local variable assignment.?  The unused variable warning occurs because the preprocessor conditionals that guard the local variable are not consistent with the usage of the local variable.? ?

Summary: Embedded Coder might generate uncompilable code for imported preprocessor definitions
Description: If a model uses imported preprocessor definitions as variant control parameters, Embedded Coder might generate uncompilable code. The uncompilable code?occurs because the preprocessor checks for the imported preprocessor definitions (macros) in the model_types.h  file even though those definitions are in the model_private.h  file.??

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code may occur when virtual blocks reorder the input data to reduced block
Description: Incorrect generated code may occur for a model that meets the following conditions:?   The optimization parameters Block Reduction  and Signal Storage?Reuse  are set to on .? Block reduction  removes a block in an active code path. Blocks?that are on?dead code paths are not susceptible to this bug. The input data to the reduced block is not in the same order as the data source. This could happen for a model that contains a Mux block or a Bus Creator block that outputs a virtual bus. The input to the Mux block or Bus Creator block are elements of a single buffer. These elements could be the?result of a Demux block or a Bus Selector block. The blocks participating in the above code path are part of the same atomic subsystem.  In the incorrect generated code, the buffer of the block driving the reduced block?may be?incorrectly reused?such that?another block overwrites the value before all uses of the original block execute. The?workaround section contains an attached example?model that?demonstrates this bug. ?In the example model, the demux/mux structure reorders elements of the Sum block before they are input to the Gain block.??In the generated code, the Gain block is reduced because it has a value of 1 . ??The Subtract block?requires the results of the Sum and Product?blocks. The Product block reuses the?Sum block?buffer. As a result, the Subtract block incorrectly uses the second element of the result of the?Product block instead of the second element of the result of the Sum block. The code snippet below shows this incorrect code (generated using R2014a without the workaround): ? ?for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {  ? ? g1475400_Y.Out1[i] = g1475400_U.In1[i] + g1475400_ConstP.Constant_Value[i]; // Sum Block output   ? ? g1475400_Y.Out3[i] = g1475400_Y.Out1[i] + -4.0;  ? ? g1475400_Y.Out1[i] *= g1475400_U.In2[i]; // Overwritten by Product Block output   ? ? g1475400_Y.Out2[i] = g1475400_Y.Out1[i] + -3.0;  ? }    ? tmp = g1475400_Y.Out1[1]; // Ends up getting result of Product Block rather than Sum Block  ? for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {  ? ? g1475400_Y.Out1[i] = (tmp - g1475400_Y.Out1[i]) + -2.0;  ? }      Examples of blocks that can be removed by block reduction - these will all be cases where the actual data is unchanged by the block calculation:  Gain blocks with a non-tunable value of 1   Concatenate blocks with a single input Absolute value blocks with an unsigned  input datatype Reshape blocks Shift block with shift amount of 0  Permute block with dimensions specified for no permutation Single input Logical AND / OR block Bias block with bias amount of 0  Switch block with non-tunable constant threshold and control port is constant? Delay block with delay 0
Workaround: To avoid this bug, you can use the attached patch to disable some optimizations?pertaining to?virtual bus selection. ?To do so, insert the p-file in the MATLAB path and each time you start MATLAB, enter the following in the MATLAB Command Window:run?workaround_g1475400.pDisabling these?optimizations?may result in more memory usage and data copies for virtual buses, but the code efficiency for demux/mux operations?does not change.Alternatively, you can?set the Block Reduction parameter to off. Changing this parameter?setting will likely result in much more memory usage and data copies than the using the attached patch.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016a Update 7, R2016b Update 6, R2017a

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Code generation for a division with a constant input may be incorrect
Description: Generating code for a division when the denominator is a constant may result in incorrect generated code.  To observe incorrect generated code, all the following must be true:  Inline invariant signals  must be enabled from the Configuration Parameters > Optimization > Signals and Parameters  pane The optimization Remove code that protects against division arithmetic exceptions  must be enabled from the?Configuration Parameters > Optimization  pane The division operation must be set to Saturate on integer overflow  The denominator must be a signed positive constant The numerator and division output type must be unsigned
Workaround: There are three known workarounds to avoid this bug:Set the default parameter behavior to Tunable in Configuration Parameters > Optimization > Signals and ParametersDisable the optimization Remove code that protects against division arithmetic exceptions in Configuration Parameters > OptimizationEnsure the numerator and denominator inputs to the division have the same signedness (either both are signed or both are unsigned)

Summary: Crash if?model reference hierarchy?uses data object through multiple handle variables
Description: Because data classes (such as Simulink.Signal  and Simulink.Parameter ) are handle classes, instead of creating two separate?data objects, this code creates?two handles?to the same object: >> A = Simulink.Parameter(5);?  >> B = A;  In general, if A and B are both referenced by a model this will result in an error. ?However, if A  and B ?are both referenced in a hierarchy of referenced models, MATLAB can crash.
Workaround: ?To?create a true copy of a data object, use the copy function:>> A = Simulink.Parameter(5);?>> B = copy(A);??
